1: ʿad-ʾānâ YHWH tišĕkāḥēnî nĕṣaḥ (ʿaḏ-ʾānâ YHWH tišĕḵāḥēnî nĕṣaḥ): 'How long, O LORD? Will you forget me forever?' The double question ʿad-ʾānâ / ʿad-ʾānâ ('how long / how long') opens Psalm 13 in anaphora — the same opening repeats in Psalms 13:1-2 a total of four times, creating a rhetorical escalation of anguish. The verb šākaḥ ('forget') is the same verb the wicked apply to God in Psalms 10:11; here the psalmist fears it may be true of himself. The adverb nĕṣaḥ ('forever, to perpetuity') intensifies: will the abandonment never end? This is honest lament theology — the question is addressed to God, not denied.
2: ʿad-ʾānâ ʾāśît ʿēṣôt bĕnapšî (ʿaḏ-ʾānâ ʾāśîṯ ʿēṣôṯ ḇĕnapšî): 'How long must I take counsel in my soul?' The four 'how long' questions span two verses: (1) divine forgetting, (2) divine hiding, (3) internal anguish ('counsel in my soul'), (4) enemy triumph. The noun ʿēṣôt ('counsel, plans') combined with yāgôn bĕlibî, 'sorrow in my heart,' shows the psalmist at an inner limit — exhausted by trying to think his way through the crisis. The rise of the enemy — yārûm ʾôyĕbî ʿālay ('my enemy is exalted over me') — is the external dimension of the same abandonment.
3: habbîṭâ ʿănēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhāy (habbîṭâ ʿănēnî YHWH ʾĕlōhāy): 'Consider and answer me, O LORD my God.' The three imperatives — habbîṭâ ('look!'), ʿănēnî ('answer me!'), hāʾîrâ ʿênay ('light up my eyes!') — are the psalm's petition block. The phrase pen-ʾîšan hammāwet, 'lest I sleep the death sleep,' uses sleep as a euphemism for death; the dimming of eyes in death is contrasted with the eyes 'lit up' by divine attention. The possessive ʾĕlōhāy ('my God') is intimate — even in the darkest lament, the covenant relationship is assumed.
4: pen-yōʾmar ʾôyĕbî yĕkoltîw (pen-yōʾmar ʾôyĕbî yĕḵoltîw): 'lest my enemy say, 'I have prevailed over him.'' The psalmist's concern is not merely personal survival but the theological stakes: if he falls, the enemy's gloating — yāgîlû ṣārāyw kî ʾemmôṭ, 'his adversaries rejoice when I am moved' — will be a public refutation of trust in YHWH. The logic is the same as Moses' appeal to YHWH's reputation in Exodus 32:12 and Numbers 14:13-16: the outcome of the psalmist's situation reflects on the credibility of YHWH's protection.
5: waʾănî bĕḥasdĕkā bāṭaḥtî (waʾănî ḇĕḥasdĕḵā ḇāṭaḥtî): 'But I have trusted in your steadfast love.' The waw-adversative waʾănî ('but as for me') marks the psalm's dramatic turn: from lament (Psalms 13:1-2) and petition (Psalms 13:3-4) to trust and praise (Psalms 13:5-6). The perfect bāṭaḥtî ('I have trusted') indicates an established disposition — not a new resolution but a prior settled stance. The noun ḥesed ('steadfast love, covenant loyalty') is YHWH's defining character; to trust in it is to rest on the most reliable foundation available. The result: yāgēl libbî bîšûʿātekā, 'my heart shall rejoice in your salvation.'
6: ʾāšîrâ laYHWH kî gāmal ʿālay (ʾāšîrâ laYHWH kî ḡāmal ʿālay): 'I will sing to the LORD, because he has dealt bountifully with me.' The cohortative ʾāšîrâ ('I will sing!') resolves the psalm with a vow of praise. The verb gāmal ('to deal with, requite, complete') — in the qal, 'to wean, to fully develop' — here means 'to deal bountifully, to complete one's care for.' The phrase kî gāmal ʿālay projects completed deliverance from the perspective of trust: the psalmist sings before the rescue arrives, confident it will come. This gap between petition and answered prayer is bridged by ḥesed-trust, making Psalm 13 a template for faith under unanswered prayer.
